chapters.title: A long history of leaving key contributors in the dark
chapters.text:
Dorothy Johnson had just earned her BSc in Mathematics from Wilberforce University in 1929. Soon after, she met a bright young man and married him. By the end of 1932, Dorothy had wed Howard Vaughan at age twenty-two. She then took up teaching at a local high school, even though she had the grades for graduate school. Racial discrimination was still deeply rooted, with Jim Crow laws running at full strength.

The real story picks up in 1943 during World War II. The National Advisory Committee for Aeronautics shifted its focus from commercial flight design to improving the military fleet. The Federal Government poured in fresh money to fund research on existing fighters and brand new ones.

Every group in the war was building smarter weapons to outpace the enemy. America decided to tap its deep pool of talent by recruiting engineers, data analysts, and mathematicians. President Franklin D. Roosevelt saw the promise in Black intellectuals and signed Executive Orders 8802 and 9346 in 1941. The orders did not cover every American of color, but they pushed the military and federal agencies to ease racial discrimination. The road ahead was still rough for Black women.

Engineers were at the heart of airplane design, and they needed human computers and analysts to handle the flood of data coming in every day. The opening for human computers was the door that brought a wave of women into NACA. Dorothy Vaughan joined NACA in 1943 as part of the first batch of human computers.

Records that mention Black women working as human computers put the number at about fifty, but the real number may have been closer to one thousand.

The mathematicians of the West Wing were not a NASA invention. Their work predated the Sputnik challenge and the Mars landings. They were part of America's story of innovation all along. Black female computers are the unsung heroes of NASA, computer science, and aeronautics.

This summary tells a story of human brilliance that crosses lines of color and origin. You probably have more than enough to carve out your own path. Read on to see the chances you can turn into a launchpad for something great.

chapters.title: Langley Research Center opens the door for women engineers
chapters.text:
Right after the U.S. caught wind of the invasion of Poland, and with American interests in Europe on the line, President Roosevelt called for a sharp jump in airplane production and a major upgrade of the defense fleet, a campaign he called Victory through airpower. The target was 50,000 airplanes per year. Before World War II, American plane production topped out at about 90 planes per month.

Before a new design or a major upgrade went out the door, the maker sent a prototype to Langley for structural review. The Langley Memorial Aeronautical Laboratory was a civilian research site on the U.S. Army Air Corps coastal base in Virginia. Before the war, Langley had about five hundred staff. With production scaling fivefold, the personnel office scrambled. Newspaper ads, flyers, and notices went out to towns around Newport News.

Before her chance at Langley, Dorothy had a path to a master's degree. The 1938 Supreme Court ruling in Missouri ex rel. Gaines v. Canada favored Black students. The ruling forced graduate programs to admit students of any race. The reality of discrimination on campus and Dorothy's loyalty to her growing family pushed her to set that path aside.

As Margot Lee Shetterly wrote, Virginia, a state with one of the highest concentrations of scientific talent in the world, led the nation in denying education to its youth.

Dorothy spent fourteen years focused on a teaching career that paid very little. White teachers sat at the low end of the pay scale, and Black teachers earned about half of what their white peers made. School funding and facilities were poor, but Dorothy pushed other teachers to invest in the children they taught. When a new chance came to use her full mind, Dorothy did not hesitate. After fourteen years of teaching, she chose to give her children a stable future.

It would be fair to think a college degree spares young people from hard labor. For people of color, and for women in particular, that is rarely the case.

chapters.title: Good years at the Langley West Wing
chapters.text:
In Hampton, the new American war town, women filled many posts at Newport News. The city also served as a dispatch point for troops heading to Europe, since it sat on the coast. During the war, a visit to Newport News made it obvious why women held so many jobs. The men were overseas. Women in overalls ran gas stations, worked the docks, and staffed other military sites.

For Black Americans doing their part in the war effort, it was a two-front fight. The first was the war against the brutality of Germany in Europe. The second was the war against racial segregation, which had cut a deep scar in American life. Many Black Americans thought it foolish to die on foreign soil for a democracy that, to them, hid its own dark side.

Black soldiers faced humiliation when they came home. That treatment made it harder for young men to enlist, since the abuse at home and the fighting abroad took a heavy toll.

Dorothy spent her first day at Langley filling out paperwork and getting her work tag. She was sent to the West Wing, where the Black computers were housed. The work was mostly mental, so the white supervisors in the wing eased up on enforcing the segregation rules. The early experience for Dorothy and the other computers was not a total joy. There were separate bathrooms, and a sign on a table at the back of the West Wing cafeteria read Black Computers. Even so, Dorothy and the other Black workers in the unit had to admit their new conditions were better than their old jobs.

They wore their work clothes like armor, as Margot Lee Shetterly put it, and they wielded their work like a weapon against the assumption of inferiority because of race or gender.

New computers, of any race, went through orientation on the work at Langley. The training covered techniques, equations, conversions, and the Reynolds number for wind tunnels. Dorothy and the other new hires also took a two-class-a-week course in aerodynamics. The course gave the women a feel for the principles that let a metal machine with an engine take to the sky.

chapters.title: The war ends, the country shifts, and new heights appear
chapters.text:
The war had run for years, and Dorothy was settling into the rhythm of Newport News. After months of boarding with a kind older Black couple, she signed a lease on a property in Newsome Park. The housing project was built to handle the wartime surge of workers, and families poured in from across Virginia. The 1,200-unit project filled up fast.

Langley hours were long, and Dorothy barely had time for herself, so she could visit her family in Farmville only a few times a month. Her husband Howard moved around for work, so she brought her kids to the new home at Newsome Park. With eighteen-hour days, six days a week, a babysitter was a must. Dorothy also enrolled her children in schools tied to the Newport News war community.

The point of fighting wars is to bring peace in the end.

News of the U.S. victory reached the country on V-J Day, August 15, 1945. V-J stood for Victory over Japan. Within three weeks, troops began arriving back at Newport News and other ports. The new reality forced the Federal Government to lay off many of the temporary hires.

Apart from Newsome Park, the temporary housing around Langley had to be torn down. Families had to move again and chase a small pool of jobs. Returning soldiers looking for work became the toughest competition.

There was a lingering fear that Langley would shrink now that the war was over, but the facility stayed busy. Newport News turned into a central military hub for the country. The U.S. Air Force, which had been the U.S. Army Air Corps, set up a base there.

Change is constant, and staying at your best helps you meet it.

Dorothy was confirmed as a permanent civil service appointee in 1946. In 1951, she became the lead supervisor of the Black computers in the West Wing, a post she had been filling on an acting basis for several years.

chapters.title: A fast race with outside pressure
chapters.text:
Russia soon caught up to the U.S. in air combat. The MIG-15s in the Soviet fleet shot down American pilots over the North and South Korean border. Communist propaganda picked up steam, and a New York couple was investigated and sentenced to death for passing secrets to the Soviet Union.

The politics of the period gave pro-segregation politicians a tool. They branded anyone who pushed back against government policy as a communist. Newport News came under FBI attention, and federal agents started dropping in on engineers, computers, and other staff at Langley.

Langley's leaders let a few people go for their loud opposition to segregation. The U.S. still wanted to rule the skies, so funding and staff for Langley became a top priority. Major Black contributors who helped build planes that broke the sound barrier stayed out of the public eye, but word spread through Black communities that Dorothy needed sharp minds.

As Margot Lee Shetterly wrote, Senator Harry Byrd slapped the label Communist on anyone or anything that threatened his idea of traditional American customs, which included white supremacy.

One of Dorothy's new hires broke a racial line. Langley's habit was to keep women as computers and men as engineers. Mary Jackson, the new hire in question, was strong with numbers and had the nerve to defend her work. That mix got her noticed and quietly flagged for bigger chances.

Langley liked to think of itself as a place that avoided red tape, where a good idea from a cafeteria worker could get a fair hearing if it was strong enough.

Another hire, Katherine Goble, turned out to be a major asset. Henry Pearson, a branch chief in the Flight Research Division, asked Dorothy to lend him one of her computers. She agreed. Two weeks in, after Katherine had wrapped up the hardest calculations, Dorothy went to see Pearson.

Dorothy pushed for Katherine's return, a permanent appointment, and a pay raise to match. Pearson refused to let her go, and he agreed to the organizational terms Dorothy had laid out.

chapters.title: The space race and the new push into the stars
chapters.text:
Around 1950, electronic calculators from companies like IBM and Bell Laboratories started showing up in research centers. Langley was among the first to try the new tools for working out supersonic flight dynamics. The machines made mistakes, could only run one string of numbers at a time, and cost a lot. The makers offered night classes at Langley, and Dorothy signed up. She pushed her computers to do the same. Around the same time, Mary Jackson, a former computer, was breaking new ground under her new boss, Kaz Czarnecki. She took an engineering course at his suggestion.

NACA was set up after the first World War to close the gap in American aviation. By World War II, the U.S. had become a force in the sky. The sense of dominance ended when Russia launched Sputnik in 1957.

The shock of waking up to a working satellite from another country is hard to overstate, as a 1946 RAND Corporation proposal put it.

The Sputnik moment set off a chain of changes at NACA. One of them was the end of the computer pool in the East Wing. Dorothy worried about the future of herself and the other West Wing computers, given the color of their skin. In October 1958, the Federal Government took a bold step that reshaped NACA and gave it the job of leading the country's space program. NACA was reborn as NASA, the National Aeronautics and Space Administration.

Once you take the first step, anything becomes possible.

Before the order that built NASA, NACA leadership circulated a memo ending all racial discrimination at the Langley Research Center. Only Dorothy and eight other mathematicians were left in the computer pool NASA inherited, as the West Wing was closed along with NACA. The change went beyond an organizational move. It marked a turning point for every woman computer at Langley.

chapters.title: A late but earned win
chapters.text:
Katherine Goble, the computer Dorothy had sent to the Flight Research Division under Pearson, played a huge role in the research behind space flight. She later changed her name to the better-known Katherine Johnson after marrying Army Captain Jim Johnson.

As Margot Lee Shetterly put it, you can't change history. These things happened the way they did. What you can change is how you look at it and how you understand that it takes the good moments and the difficult moments to move forward.

The space race started rocky for the NASA team, but it found its footing in 1960. The country was starting to set aside its bias against women and people of color. The public story of women's role in the space race helped drive that change. There was an orbital flight by John Glenn's team, then a moon landing. America won the space race when it decided to face its own internal biases.

The good part of breaking a barrier is that it stays broken.

A quick fact: research by Daniel Amen and colleagues in 2017 found that women tend to show higher brain activity than men across many regions.

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
For many people, the movie or book Hidden Figures is the first time they hear about the huge role Black women played in space exploration. The story is uplifting because it shows that merit outweighs race, sex, and status when picking the right people. Being female is already a hurdle that leads to bias at work and in ideas. Add being Black on top of that, and the hurdle grows. The stories in Hidden Figures urge women to keep pushing for greatness, no matter the roadblocks.

Of the many Black women who worked as computers at the time, Katherine Johnson became the most famous. She said in interviews that she was not the brightest of the group. Her later fame showed just how much Black women had contributed to science.

Dorothy Vaughan served as NACA's first Black manager in the West Wing for over ten years. Mary Jackson came in as a mathematician and became the first Black female engineer at NASA.

Dorothy Vaughan once said, what I changed, I could; what I couldn't, I endured.

The women of NACA and NASA's West Wing at Langley joined during the war and gave their all. Some of them, Black and white, stayed for over thirty years in the service of science. Knowing about these contributions makes it easier to see how important women are to modern life.

A small practice to try.

As a team member or leader, make a point of noticing the role women play in getting things done. It can unlock the kind of growth your home, team, business, or organization needs. A few practical tips follow.

Go big with appreciation, since people feel seen when their work is announced to a wider audience.

Listen actively to their ideas and suggestions, and credit their creativity.

Create more chances for women, and chances are they will deliver.

Finished reading? Nice work.
